attributes.stem:
A 78-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with worsening breathlessness over three months. He reports orthopnoea requiring four pillows, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, and ankle swelling. He can now only walk a few steps before becoming breathless. He has had three hospital admissions for acute heart failure in the past six months despite optimal medical therapy.

His medical history includes ischaemic cardiomyopathy with an ejection fraction of 25%, previous myocardial infarction, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and atrial fibrillation. His medications include carvedilol, sacubitril/valsartan, spironolactone, furosemide, apixaban, metformin, and insulin.

On examination, he appears tired and short of breath at rest. His pulse rate is 88/min and irregular, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 93% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. He has elevated jugular venous pressure, bilateral coarse crackles to mid-zones, and pitting oedema to his thighs. He expresses a wish to maximise his time at home and reduce his symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is a proven benefit of early palliative care involvement in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with end-stage heart failure, characterised by worsening symptoms despite optimal medical therapy, frequent hospital admissions, and significant functional decline. The integration of palliative care alongside standard heart failure management is increasingly recognised as beneficial in such cases, as well as in patients with heart failure not for consideration of transplantation.

Improved quality of life is the most well-established benefit of early palliative care involvement in end-stage heart failure. Multiple studies have demonstrated that palliative care improves symptom control, emotional well-being, and helps patients achieve their goals of care, particularly when introduced early in the course of advanced disease.

Reduced mortality has not been demonstrated as a benefit of palliative care in heart failure. In fact, palliative care neither hastens nor prolongs death, focusing instead on quality rather than quantity of life.

Reduced medication costs is not a proven benefit of palliative care involvement. While medication review is part of comprehensive palliative care, cost reduction is not a primary outcome.

Increased survival time has not been demonstrated with palliative care involvement in heart failure. The focus is on quality of life rather than extending life.

Fewer hospital readmissions, while sometimes observed, has not been consistently demonstrated as a benefit of palliative care in heart failure. Some studies suggest a reduction in emergency department visits, but this is not considered a proven benefit.

The unpredictable trajectory of heart failure makes it particularly important to integrate palliative care early, allowing for advance care planning and establishment of goals of care while the patient can actively participate in decision-making. This approach helps align care with patient preferences and improves satisfaction with care for both patients and families.
learningPoints: Early palliative care involvement in end-stage heart failure improves quality of life and reduces symptom burden.
Palliative care does not shorten life expectancy in end-stage heart failure.
Regular review of advance care planning is essential in end-stage heart failure due to unpredictable disease trajectory.
references: NHFA CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines Working Group, Atherton JJ, Sindone A, et al. National Heart Foundation of Australia and Cardiac Society of Australia and New Zealand: Guidelines for the Prevention, Detection, and Management of Heart Failure in Australia 2018. Heart Lung Circ. 2018;27(10):1123-1208. doi:10.1016/j.hlc.2018.06.1042 Available online at ; https://www.heartlungcirc.org/article/S1443-9506(18)31777-3/fulltext

Adler ED, Goldfinger JZ, Kalman J, Park ME, Meier DE. Palliative care in the treatment of advanced heart failure. Circulation. 2009;120(25):2597-2606. doi:10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.109.869123 Available online at ; https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/full/10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.109.869123

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
